---
title: "Epidemic Curve"
id: "OUTBREAK_VE_DEMO-se02-sec01-sp02"
section_id: "OUTBREAK_VE_DEMO-se02-sec01"
session_id: "OUTBREAK_VE_DEMO-se02"
toc: True
---











<!-- IMPORT_START -->

Data tables provide detail, but visualisations reveal patterns that are difficult to see in raw numbers.

## Comparing Risk Between Groups

```{r}
group <- c("Vaccinated", "Unvaccinated")
cases <- c(5, 25)
population <- c(500, 500)
risk <- cases / population
barplot(risk, names.arg = group, col = c("steelblue", "tomato"))
```

## Understanding the Epidemic Curve

![Epidemic curve showing progression of cases over time](../../resources/images/epidemic-curve.png){width='70%'}
Figure 1. Outbreak progression by group

## Reading Visual Data

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Bar Chart
Highlights differences in risk between groups\

## Epidemic Curve
Shows how the outbreak evolves over time\

## Combined Insight
Both views are needed for full interpretation

:::
## Reflection

::: {.callout-tip}
**Self-check:** Why is it useful to view both a chart and a table?

**Suggested answer**

Each provides different insights --- charts show patterns, tables show detail
:::

## Key Message

::: {.callout-tip}
Visualisation supports faster and clearer interpretation of complex data
:::

<!-- IMPORT_END -->
<!-- Standard workflow: content imported from Word -->
<!-- Advanced users may edit directly -->

<!-- START_NAVIGATION -->


```{=html}
<div class="custom-page-navigation">
    <div class="nav-prev">
        
        <a href="OUTBREAK_VE_DEMO-se02-sec01-sp01.qmd">
            <span class="nav-label">← Previous</span>
            <span class="nav-title">Risk by Vaccination Status</span>
        </a>
        
    </div>
    <div class="nav-next">
        
        <a href="OUTBREAK_VE_DEMO-se02-sec01-sp03.qmd">
            <span class="nav-label">Next →</span>
            <span class="nav-title">Using R for Public Health Visualisation</span>
        </a>
        
    </div>
</div>
```

<!-- END_NAVIGATION -->
